title: In parallel, assess the likely suitability of the Google AI agents technical guide recommendations for a company backend like Cursorvers.Inc. Focus on architectural fit, operational risks, vendor lock-in, security/compliance, and what should or should not become the core backend. Return a concise re...
summary_text: **Findings (ordered by severity)** 1. **Critical: Don’t make agent runtime the core transactional backend.** Google’s own guidance says predictable/structured workloads are often better as non-agentic flows; agents are for open-ended, tool-using tasks. For a backend like Cursorvers.Inc, core domain paths (auth, billing, ledger, entitlement, order/state transitions) should stay deterministic. 2. **Critical: Compliance/data-retention traps can block regulated core use.** Vertex AI zero-data-retention has exceptions: prompt/output retention for some grounding modes (Google Search/Maps) and prompt logging scenarios. If sensitive data passes through these paths, this can violate strict retention policies. 3. **High: Security posture is strong but easy to misconfigure operationally.** Agent Engine supports VPC-SC/CMEK/DRZ/HIPAA, but deployment order and perimeter setup matter; there are caveats (for example, perimeter timing and reduced logging options in some VPC-SC setups). This is risky for core systems without strong platform controls. 4. **High: Vendor lock-in risk is material at runtime/state layer.** Lock-in concentrates if you depend on Agent Engine-managed sessions/memory/code-execution and Gemini-specific managed capabilities. Also, Agent Engine is opinionated (notably Python-focused and no hosting of custom MCP servers), which can constrain backend evolution. 5. **Medium: Operational/cost risk rises quickly with advanced agent patterns.** Google highlights loop/swarm-style patterns can create runaway cost/latency risks. Quotas, preview features, and timeout constraints can become production bottlenecks if agent workflows are placed on critical paths. 6. **Medium (positive): There is a portability path if designed intentionally.** ADK is positioned as model/deployment-agnostic, and A2A is an open protocol (Linux Foundation), which helps reduce lock-in if you keep clean boundaries and avoid provider-specific state as system-of-record. **Brief recommendation** Use Google AI agent recommendations as an **AI capability layer**, not the company’s core backend. - **Should become core:** deterministic business services, canonical data stores, policy/authorization, workflow engine, observability/compliance controls. - **Should not become core:** agent memory/session stores as source-of-truth, LLM-orchestrated flows for high-stakes transactions, retention-constrained grounding paths for regulated data. - **Practical target architecture:** hybrid model with cloud-agnostic backend core + bounded agent services (ADK/A2A/MCP) behind strict policy gates and fallback deterministic paths. Sources: - https://docs.cloud.google.com/architecture/choose-agentic-ai-architecture-components - https://docs.cloud.google.com/architecture/choose-design-pattern-agentic-ai-system - https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/agent-engine/overview - https://docs.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/vertex-ai-zero-data-retention - https://docs.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/docs/general/vpc-service-controls - https://google.github.io/adk-docs/ - https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/
